title: Compliance by Rule/Policy Category

compliance.assurance_rationale: Monitors device configuration compliance status against Catalyst Center policies, providing evidence of configuration baseline enforcement.
compliance.controlObjective: Breaks non-compliance out by `complianceType` so the organisation can see which control settings fail most often, supporting focused remediation under CM-6.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search or dashboard panel `UC-5.13.31` (violations and affected device counts by category) with drilldown; attach to the CM evidence pack for each review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Demonstrates network device configuration compliance monitoring as part of firewall and router configuration standards.
compliance.controlObjective: Clarifies which classes of device configuration (for example image versus running config) drive failures, helping prove coverage of 1.1.1 in practice.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Dashboard export and optional lookup table of remediation owners per `complianceType` for QSA walkthroughs.

dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:compliance` from the `compliance` modular input in `TA_cisco_catalyst` (Splunkbase 7538). Same data feed as UC-5.13.28. Key dimension: `complianceType` (RUNNING_CONFIG, IMAGE, PSIRT, EOX, NETWORK_SETTINGS) — Catalyst Center's categorisation of what kind of compliance check failed.
spl:
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:compliance" complianceStatus="NON_COMPLIANT"
| stats dc(deviceName) as affected_devices by complianceType
| eventstats sum(affected_devices) as total_violations
| eval pct=round(affected_devices*100/total_violations,1)
| sort -affected_devices
description: Breaks down non-compliance by policy category — RUNNING_CONFIG, IMAGE, PSIRT, EOX, NETWORK_SETTINGS — showing which type of compliance check accounts for the most violations, so remediation effort is directed at the largest gap rather than spread thinly across all categories.
value: UC-5.13.28 tells you *how many* devices are non-compliant. This UC tells you *why*. If 80% of violations are RUNNING_CONFIG, the team needs to push golden templates. If 80% are IMAGE, the priority is a firmware upgrade campaign. If 80% are PSIRT, there's a critical vulnerability exposure. Each compliance type has a different remediation owner, timeline, and cost — this breakdown ensures budget and effort go to the category that moves the compliance percentage the most.
implementation: Same `compliance` input as UC-5.13.28. Filters to NON_COMPLIANT only. Groups by `complianceType` to show which policy families have the most violations. Schedule weekly alongside UC-5.13.28.
visualization: (1) Pie or donut: affected_devices by complianceType — shows the relative share of each violation type. (2) Table: complianceType, affected_devices, pct — sorted by count. (3) Drilldown: click RUNNING_CONFIG → filter UC-5.13.33 (Violation Detail) to show the specific configuration deltas. Click IMAGE → link to UC-5.13.56 (Firmware Non-Compliance). (4) Stacked bar comparison: COMPLIANT vs NON_COMPLIANT by complianceType (requires adding COMPLIANT to the search).

references.title: Catalyst Center Intent API — Compliance endpoint
NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 — CM-6 Configuration Settings

knownFalsePositives:
**NOT_APPLICABLE compliance types inflating the denominator.** If NOT_APPLICABLE devices are counted alongside COMPLIANT and NON_COMPLIANT, the per-type breakdown becomes misleading. This UC filters to NON_COMPLIANT only, avoiding this. But be aware that some device families don't support certain compliance types (e.g., IMAGE compliance for APs), so the affected-device count for those types will be naturally zero.

**IMAGE non-compliance dominated by devices awaiting scheduled upgrade.** Devices in the SWIM upgrade queue may show IMAGE non-compliance even though the upgrade is planned. Distinguish by cross-referencing with UC-5.13.57 (Upgrade Progress). Suppress by tracking IMAGE non-compliance separately from RUNNING_CONFIG non-compliance — IMAGE is often an operations issue, while RUNNING_CONFIG is a security/change-management issue.

**PSIRT non-compliance for low-severity advisories.** Catalyst Center may flag PSIRT non-compliance for informational advisories that don't require immediate action. Distinguish by checking the advisory severity in UC-5.13.34. Suppress by filtering PSIRT violations to severity="CRITICAL" or "HIGH" only.

**EOX non-compliance for devices with approved lifecycle exceptions.** End-of-life devices may be approved to run past their EOX date with documented risk acceptance. Distinguish by checking the `catalyst_compliance_exceptions` lookup. Suppress by filtering excepted devices.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• UC-5.13.28 (Compliance Status Overview) must be operational — same `compliance` data feed.
• Understand the Catalyst Center compliance type taxonomy:
  - **RUNNING_CONFIG**: device running configuration vs golden template. Most common type. Failures indicate configuration drift — someone made a manual CLI change or the template wasn't pushed.
  - **IMAGE**: device firmware version vs designated golden image. Failures indicate firmware non-compliance. See UC-5.13.56 for firmware-specific analysis.
  - **PSIRT**: device firmware vs Cisco PSIRT (security advisory) database. Failures indicate the device is affected by a known vulnerability. See UC-5.13.34–39.
  - **EOX**: device hardware/software vs Cisco End-of-Life announcements. Failures indicate the device is past its support lifecycle.
  - **NETWORK_SETTINGS**: device settings vs Catalyst Center network design settings. Less common; depends on Catalyst Center design configuration.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Same `compliance` input as UC-5.13.28. No additional configuration.

Confirm multiple compliance types are present:
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:compliance" earliest=-24h
| stats dc(deviceName) as devices by complianceType, complianceStatus
| sort complianceType
```
You should see multiple `complianceType` values with both COMPLIANT and NON_COMPLIANT status.

Step 2 — Create the search and report
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:compliance" complianceStatus="NON_COMPLIANT"
| stats dc(deviceName) as affected_devices by complianceType
| eventstats sum(affected_devices) as total_violations
| eval pct=round(affected_devices*100/total_violations,1)
| sort -affected_devices
```

Why `dc(deviceName)` not `count`: the search window may cover multiple polls, and the same device appears in every poll. `dc(deviceName)` gives the actual number of unique non-compliant devices per type.

Why `eventstats sum` for the percentage: computes the total across all types in a single pass, so each row shows its share of the total violation pool.

For a COMPLIANT vs NON_COMPLIANT breakdown per type (more comprehensive):
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:compliance"
| where complianceStatus IN ("COMPLIANT","NON_COMPLIANT")
| stats dc(eval(if(complianceStatus="COMPLIANT",deviceName,null()))) as compliant dc(eval(if(complianceStatus="NON_COMPLIANT",deviceName,null()))) as non_compliant by complianceType
| eval total=compliant+non_compliant
| eval compliant_pct=round(compliant*100/total,1)
| sort compliant_pct
```

Schedule as Report: weekly (cron `0 7 * * 1`), output alongside UC-5.13.28 on the Compliance Posture dashboard.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Compare the per-type breakdown with **Catalyst Center > Compliance** filtered by type. The device counts per type should match.

(b) Verify all expected compliance types appear. If only RUNNING_CONFIG shows, other compliance types may not be configured in Catalyst Center.

(c) Sum `affected_devices` across all types. Note: the total may be LESS than UC-5.13.28's non-compliant count (because UC-5.13.28 counts unique devices, and one device may fail multiple types) or MORE (because `dc(deviceName)` is per-type here).

(d) Cross-reference IMAGE violations with UC-5.13.56 (Firmware Non-Compliance) — the device counts should approximately match.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard placement (on the "Compliance Posture" dashboard, next to UC-5.13.28's donut chart):
• Pie chart: affected_devices by complianceType.
• Table with drilldown: click a type → filter UC-5.13.33 (Violation Detail) to that complianceType.

Remediation prioritisation:
1. Review which compliance type has the most violations.
2. Assign remediation ownership by type:
   - RUNNING_CONFIG → Change Management (golden template push)
   - IMAGE → Operations (firmware upgrade campaign, UC-5.13.56)
   - PSIRT → Security Operations (vulnerability remediation, UC-5.13.34–39)
   - EOX → Asset Management (hardware refresh planning)
   - NETWORK_SETTINGS → Network Architecture (design alignment)
3. Track per-type compliance_pct over time: `| timechart span=1w dc(eval(if(complianceStatus="COMPLIANT",deviceName,null()))) as compliant by complianceType`.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Only one compliance type appears** — other types may not be configured in Catalyst Center. Check **Catalyst Center > Compliance > Policies** for enabled policy types.

• **IMAGE violations dominate** — expected in environments with delayed firmware campaigns. Track IMAGE separately from RUNNING_CONFIG for operations vs security prioritisation.

• **PSIRT type is absent** — PSIRT compliance checking may require a separate Catalyst Center license or feature enablement. Check **Catalyst Center > Security Advisories**.

• **Total affected_devices across types > total non-compliant devices in UC-5.13.28** — a single device can be non-compliant for multiple types. This is correct — it shows the total remediation workload, not the unique device count.

• **EOX violations for devices with planned replacements** — use the `catalyst_compliance_exceptions` lookup to flag these as 'acknowledged with replacement date' rather than open violations.

• **NETWORK_SETTINGS type shows high violations** — this type checks device settings against Catalyst Center's design intent. If the design settings are incomplete or incorrect, many devices may appear non-compliant. Review the design settings in **Catalyst Center > Design > Network Settings**.

• **Compliance type names changed after Catalyst Center upgrade** — check `| stats values(complianceType)` and update dashboard labels.

• **No data for some types on certain device families** — expected. APs typically don't support IMAGE or EOX compliance. The absence is correct, not an error.
grandmaExplanation: We sort the configuration problems by type — is the issue a wrong software version, a changed setting, or a security vulnerability? This tells your team whether they need to push an update, fix a setting, or patch a security hole, instead of treating all problems the same way.
